one_line_takeaway: Replacing one-to-one output matching with a node-level contrastive objective plus spectral graph augmentations makes inductive-GNN model stealing substantially more query-efficient and accurate, beating the sole prior baseline (Shen et al. 2021) on most settings and cutting the queries needed by about half.
key_finding: The contrastive + spectral-augmentation attack outperforms the Shen et al. (2021) baseline across the large majority of dataset/architecture/response combinations, for both theft (accuracy/F1) and reconnaissance (fidelity), while needing fewer queries. Headline gains: with a GIN target and SAGE surrogate on the Amazon Photos dataset, accuracy improves by roughly 36.8 percentage points (abstract). In Type II attacks against a GIN target, the SAGE surrogate on Amazon gains 18.7 pp prediction accuracy and 35.8 pp embedding accuracy over the baseline, and embedding fidelity jumps from 46.3% to 81.1%. On query efficiency, the method reaches the baseline's best accuracy/fidelity on prediction and embedding responses using only ~50% of the queries the baseline needs. The Gaussian-noise defense (injecting noise into embeddings/projections) only marginally degrades the surrogate, so the attack still yields a useful stolen model, and the noise also hurts the API's legitimate utility.
core_contribution: The paper introduces a new unsupervised model-stealing attack against inductive Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Instead of the prior state of the art's approach of simply regressing the surrogate model's outputs onto the victim's per-node responses (one-to-one supervised matching via RMSE), the authors train the surrogate with a node-level contrastive objective: for each queried node they form a positive pair between the victim's response and the surrogate's embedding of an augmented view of that node, while pushing apart embeddings of different nodes (negative pairs, both across the two models and within the surrogate). To generate augmented views they adapt spectral graph augmentations (spectral graph cropping and graph-frequency-component reordering, forming an 'optimal contrastive pair' where high-frequency spectral differences exceed low-frequency ones, plus feature masking, edge dropping, and edge perturbation). The stated goal is to extract far more information per query from the victim's outputs, so the attack matches or beats the baseline while sending fewer queries. The authors are explicit that their method plugs into the attack framework and taxonomy of Shen et al. (2021), which is prior cited work, not their own contribution; their novelty is the surrogate-training procedure (contrastive loss + spectral augmentations), not the overall framework, threat model, or the IDGL-based structure-inference step which they reuse from prior work.
threat_model: Black-box model extraction. The adversary has only query access to a deployed inductive GNN's API and knows nothing about the target's parameters or architecture, cannot interfere with its training, and cannot see the training graph. The adversary submits a query graph GQ and receives one of three response types per node: a node-embedding matrix (H), a predicted-posterior-probability matrix (Theta), or a 2-D t-SNE projection matrix (Upsilon). The query graph is assumed drawn from the same distribution as the victim's training graph (same public dataset). Two attack types are considered: Type I, where the adversary possesses the query graph's adjacency structure; and Type II, where the adjacency matrix is unknown and must be reconstructed (via the IDGL framework, following prior work) before querying. Adversary goals follow the theft-vs-reconnaissance taxonomy: theft aims to replicate task performance (measured by accuracy/F1), reconnaissance aims to faithfully mimic the victim across inputs (measured by fidelity), enabling downstream attacks such as adversarial-example generation or training-data extraction.
limitations: The authors state the attack is limited to targets that expose node-level outputs; extending to graph-level or link-prediction tasks would require different augmentations and is left as future work. The Type II missing-graph-structure reconstruction is not novel here: it directly reuses the IDGL-based approach of Shen et al., and the authors flag that a structure-reconstruction method tailored to contrastive surrogate training is future work. Reviewer-noted caveats: the strong same-distribution assumption (query graph sampled from the same dataset as the victim's training graph) is a favorable setting that may not hold against real APIs; gains on the t-SNE projection response are weaker and inconsistent (for GIN/SAGE surrogates the projection task needs closer to 30% of queries, not 50%, to match the baseline, and several projection-task cells show the baseline winning); and the only defense evaluated is a single Gaussian-noise scheme on one dataset, so robustness against stronger active defenses is untested.

quantitative_results: Abstract headline: up to +36.8 percentage-point accuracy gain over baseline for a GIN target with SAGE surrogate on Amazon Photos
Type II, GIN target, SAGE surrogate, Amazon: prediction accuracy +18.7 pp over baseline
Type II, GIN target, SAGE surrogate, Amazon: embedding accuracy +35.8 pp over baseline
Type II, GIN target, SAGE surrogate, Amazon: embedding fidelity rises from 46.3% to 81.1%
Query efficiency: matches baseline's best accuracy/fidelity on prediction and embedding responses using ~50% of the baseline's queries
Type I, GIN target, GIN surrogate, DBLP embedding: accuracy 0.770 (ours) vs 0.705 (Shen et al.)
Type I, GIN target, GIN surrogate, Citeseer prediction: accuracy 0.820 (ours) vs 0.773 (Shen et al.)
Type I, GIN target, GIN surrogate, Coauthor prediction: accuracy 0.895 (ours) vs 0.733 (Shen et al.)
Type I, GIN target, GAT surrogate, ACM prediction fidelity: nearly +20 pp; Original GIN classification accuracy on Amazon 0.819 (Table 2)
baselines_compared: Shen et al. (2021) [33] - the only prior model-stealing method applicable to inductive GNNs; re-run by the authors on disjoint data splits for a fair comparison
Gaussian-noise injection defense (following Shen et al.) evaluated as a countermeasure on ACM
